claim: The City of Larkhaven says all municipal buildings now run on 100% renewable electricity through a mix of rooftop solar and a utility green-power contract.
teaching_focus: basic search to open to verdict flow
notes: A simple confirmatory scenario where two institutional sources align.

sources.title: Larkhaven council approves citywide renewable power contract
sources.snippet: The city says all 14 municipal buildings shifted to a renewable electricity portfolio in July. The plan combines rooftop solar generation with a certified green-power purchase agreement for remaining load.

sources.title: Utility filing details Larkhaven municipal energy mix
sources.snippet: NorthGrid's filing states Larkhaven enrolled every municipal meter in its renewable tariff and linked the account to existing rooftop solar arrays. The filing describes the combined portfolio as fully renewable on an annual matched basis.

sources.title: Column questions whether renewable accounting is 'real power'
sources.snippet: An opinion columnist argues renewable contracts are not the same as direct on-site generation. The piece does not dispute that the city's electricity purchases are contractually matched with renewable supply.

claim: A company called NeuroPeak received federal approval to sell a sticker patch that boosts memory by 40% in healthy adults.
notes: Best trajectory checks the promotional source's credibility before opening the regulator source.

sources.title: NeuroPeak patch wins federal clearance for memory enhancement
sources.snippet: A marketing article claims NeuroPeak's adhesive patch was cleared after a breakthrough trial in healthy adults. The article offers no approval number and links only to the seller's checkout page.

sources.title: Device approvals database shows no NeuroPeak listing
sources.snippet: The public approvals database contains no product called NeuroPeak and no authorized memory-enhancement patch for healthy adults. The database notes that wellness claims of this kind require substantiated clinical evidence and an authorization record.

sources.title: About page for BrainBoost Now
sources.snippet: The site's about page lists no medical board, street address, or named researchers. Domain records show the website was registered two months ago through a privacy shield.

claim: Eastport's new stadium eliminated landfill waste in its first season.
notes: The team promoted a narrow metric while the city audit covers all event waste.

sources.title: Eastport Arena declares a zero-waste first season
sources.snippet: The venue says all concession packaging was compostable and that sorting stations diverted operational waste away from landfill. The announcement presents the season as 'zero waste' without showing audit totals.

sources.title: City audit finds most event waste diverted but not all
sources.snippet: A municipal audit reports 91% diversion across the season, with the remainder going to landfill because mixed plastics and contaminated foodware could not be processed. The audit says the zero-waste label overstates the result.

sources.title: Sustainability consultant explains common zero-waste thresholds
sources.snippet: An industry explainer says some operators use 'zero waste' for systems above 90% diversion, while public reporting standards often reserve the term for no landfill disposal at all. It advises venues to publish the exact diversion rate.

claim: Researchers proved that drinking kelp tea every day reverses microplastic buildup in the human bloodstream.
teaching_focus: clean search and evidence insufficiency pattern
notes: The available sources discuss lab observations and speculation, not proof in humans.

sources.title: Lifestyle blog says kelp tea 'flushes microplastics'
sources.snippet: A blog post claims daily kelp tea reverses microplastic buildup in blood and cites unnamed 'marine detox researchers.' It links to no human trial and provides no dosage or measurement details.

sources.title: Marine nutrition lab studies algae compounds in cell cultures
sources.snippet: A university summary describes a lab experiment in which algae-derived compounds bound to plastic particles in vitro. The researchers say the work does not show removal from the human bloodstream and that no clinical study has been completed.

sources.title: Evidence review on microplastic exposure interventions
sources.snippet: A review concludes there is no established therapy proven to remove microplastics from human blood. It calls current intervention claims premature because clinical detection methods and outcomes remain unsettled.

claim: The River County water agency cut household water use by 25% last year.
notes: The number is supported after comparing the annual report with the regulator filing.

sources.title: River County agency reports 25% drop in residential demand
sources.snippet: The agency's annual report says per-household demand fell from 164 liters per person per day to 123 during the previous fiscal year. It attributes the drop to tiered pricing and leak-repair rebates.

sources.title: State utilities filing confirms River County conservation result
sources.snippet: The regulator's filing shows River County's residential demand declined by 24.8% year over year using the same billing-period methodology. The filing notes weather adjustments were minor and did not change the rounded result.

sources.title: Regional business group says water savings figure is inflated
sources.snippet: A trade association claims the agency counted population shifts instead of real conservation. The article provides no alternate dataset and does not address the regulator's audited filing.

claim: Metrovale schools banned all screens for children under age 10.
teaching_focus: open official policy after checking a rumor source
notes: The district limited non-instructional use but did not ban classroom screens.

sources.title: Parents group says Metrovale enacted a total screen ban
sources.snippet: A viral post says children under 10 can no longer use tablets, classroom displays, or computer labs in Metrovale schools. The post quotes no district policy language and links to a screenshot without a document source.

sources.title: Metrovale district publishes classroom technology guidance
sources.snippet: The district guidance limits entertainment videos and recommends shorter device sessions for early grades. It explicitly permits teacher-led use of tablets, literacy software, and interactive displays for instruction.

sources.title: Forum domain registration and moderation details
sources.snippet: The forum's operators are anonymous and the site labels itself as a 'citizen rumor board.' Posts are user-submitted and are published before moderation unless they trigger spam filters.

claim: Northbay's crime rate doubled after the city opened its safe-parking program.
teaching_focus: compare sensational framing against current dataset
notes: A short-term spike in one category is being framed as a citywide doubling.

sources.title: Commentary links safe-parking program to doubling crime
sources.snippet: An opinion piece says crime doubled near the safe-parking site and implies the citywide trend followed. The chart in the piece covers only three months of theft reports from one police beat.

sources.title: Northbay public safety dashboard annual totals
sources.snippet: The city's dashboard shows overall reported crime rose 4% year over year, not 100%. It notes a temporary cluster of vehicle thefts in one zone during the program's first quarter, followed by a decline after lighting and patrol changes.

sources.title: Regional data desk explains why local spikes can distort trend claims
sources.snippet: A data analysis article shows how comparing one unusually low quarter to a later spike can create dramatic percentages. It recommends using full-year citywide totals when making broad public-safety claims.

claim: The national transport agency banned electric scooters nationwide after batteries in three commuter trains caught fire.
notes: The rumor spread from an edited screenshot of a station notice about scooters on platforms, not a national ban.

sources.title: Post claims emergency nationwide scooter ban is already in effect
sources.snippet: The article publishes an image that appears to show a national ban notice after three train fires. It cites unnamed rail staff and provides no link to the transport agency or incident reports.

sources.title: Transport agency clarifies no national scooter ban exists
sources.snippet: The agency says there is no nationwide prohibition on electric scooters. It explains that one station temporarily restricted charging and indoor storage after a battery incident on a platform, and that the edited image circulating online is not an agency bulletin.

sources.title: TransitFlash contact and domain records
sources.snippet: The site lists no newsroom address and hides ownership behind a bulk registrar. Its archive shows multiple deleted posts that reused altered public notices to drive traffic during previous transport scares.

claim: A leaked memo proves Harbor Biologics secretly confirmed that its algae-protein drink causes liver scarring.
notes: The memo is not authenticated and the public evidence does not prove the claim.

sources.title: Anonymous account posts leaked Harbor Biologics memo
sources.snippet: A pseudonymous post shares screenshots of what it says is an internal memo about liver scarring linked to an algae-protein drink. The images contain no signatures, headers, or document controls that allow independent verification.

sources.title: Food safety authority says it has not verified the circulating memo
sources.snippet: The authority says it is reviewing adverse-event complaints but has not authenticated the memo shared online. It notes that current public reporting does not establish a confirmed causal link between the drink and liver scarring.

sources.title: Platform trace shows memo screenshots were reposted from an unverified image board
sources.snippet: A digital forensics note traces the screenshots back to an anonymous image board thread from two days earlier. The analysts say the file metadata was stripped, preventing origin confirmation.

claim: Sungrain cereal was banned because regulators found it contained twice the legal limit of a pesticide residue.
notes: One product lot was recalled voluntarily; the brand was not banned across the market.

sources.title: Posts say Sungrain cereal was banned nationwide over pesticide levels
sources.snippet: A roundup article says regulators banned Sungrain cereal after tests found residue at twice the legal limit. The piece does not identify a regulation notice number and treats a single lot action as a product-wide prohibition.

sources.title: Regulator notice describes voluntary recall of one production lot
sources.snippet: The food standards notice says one Sungrain lot was voluntarily recalled after residue exceeded the action threshold. It states the agency did not issue a nationwide ban and that other tested lots complied with residue limits.

sources.title: Retail trade bulletin explains difference between recalls and bans
sources.snippet: An industry bulletin says a recall removes specified lots from sale, while a ban blocks a product category or brand entirely. It cites the Sungrain case as an example of how social posts exaggerate lot-specific enforcement.

claim: Westhaven Public Library eliminated overdue fines for all children's materials this year.
notes: A small local policy claim with aligned official and local coverage.

sources.title: Westhaven library board votes to remove fines on children's items
sources.snippet: Board minutes say overdue fines for children's books, audiobooks, and learning kits ended on January 1. Replacement fees still apply only when items are declared lost.

sources.title: Community paper summarizes Westhaven fine-free policy
sources.snippet: The local paper reports families no longer pay late fees on children's materials under the new library policy. The article quotes the library director saying the change is permanent unless the board reverses it.

sources.title: Opinion letter argues fine-free libraries encourage carelessness
sources.snippet: A letter writer criticizes the new rule and says some residents thought all library fines disappeared. The letter does not dispute that children's materials are now exempt from overdue fees.

claim: Mushroom coffee was officially approved as an ADHD treatment after a clinical breakthrough.
notes: A wellness product is being falsely framed as a clinically approved treatment.

sources.title: Viral post says focus mushroom blend is now an approved ADHD treatment
sources.snippet: A supplement marketing blog says a mushroom coffee blend earned approval after a breakthrough focus study. It cites no approval number, dosing guidance, or prescribing information.

sources.title: Drug approvals index lists no mushroom coffee ADHD therapy
sources.snippet: The public index shows no approved ADHD treatment matching the promoted mushroom coffee product or formulation. The authority warns consumers that supplements cannot be marketed as approved prescription therapies without formal authorization.

sources.title: Site ownership details for MindFuel Daily
sources.snippet: The website is registered through a discount affiliate network and lists only a contact form, not a manufacturer or clinic. Its footer discloses that product reviews are monetized through referral links.

claim: Redfield Airport became fully energy independent after installing solar canopies over its parking lots.
teaching_focus: compare marketing language against actual coverage
notes: The solar project covers a large share of demand but not all airport operations.

sources.title: Airport celebrates solar canopy launch
sources.snippet: The airport says new parking-lot solar canopies will supply roughly 62% of annual terminal electricity demand. The release calls the project a major step toward energy independence.

sources.title: Utility interconnection filing for Redfield solar project
sources.snippet: The filing estimates annual generation will offset a majority of terminal use, while runways, hangars, and winter peak demand will still rely on grid power. It does not describe the airport as fully off-grid.

sources.title: Regional clean-energy newsletter praises airport project
sources.snippet: A newsletter says airports often use 'energy independent' as an aspirational phrase even when on-site generation covers only part of demand. It recommends reporting the exact share of total load served.

claim: A new biodegradable phone case is proven to dissolve harmlessly in the ocean within three weeks.
notes: Marketing language outruns the lab evidence.

sources.title: Startup advertises phone case that disappears in seawater in 21 days
sources.snippet: The product page says the case dissolves harmlessly in the ocean within three weeks and leaves no toxic residue. It links to a short materials note rather than a field test or certification report.

sources.title: University lab tests polymer breakdown under controlled conditions
sources.snippet: Researchers report that samples of a related polymer degraded rapidly in warm, aerated seawater tanks. The study did not test the finished phone case in open-ocean conditions and did not evaluate ecological safety claims.

sources.title: Marine materials review warns lab degradation does not prove real-world ocean behavior
sources.snippet: A review says ocean biodegradation claims require standardized field testing because temperature, salinity, fouling, and sunlight vary widely. It notes that many products degrade differently outside laboratory conditions.

claim: Metroline's redesigned bus network increased weekday ridership by 18% in its first six months.
teaching_focus: compare operator report with oversight filing
notes: A moderate factual claim with supportive but differently phrased evidence.

sources.title: Metroline says redesigned network lifted weekday ridership
sources.snippet: The transit agency says average weekday boardings rose from 112,000 to 132,300 in the first six months after the route redesign. The release describes the gain as an 18% increase.

sources.title: State transit performance filing for Metroline redesign
sources.snippet: The oversight filing reports an 18.1% increase in average weekday ridership relative to the matched pre-redesign period. The filing notes that service frequency and transfer simplification were the main drivers.

sources.title: Editorial argues ridership gains reflect fare discounts, not redesign
sources.snippet: A commentary piece says the network map is getting too much credit for ridership growth. It does not contest the measured 18% increase, only the explanation for why it occurred.

claim: North District schools switched all student lunches to 100% organic ingredients.
notes: The district expanded organic sourcing but did not make every ingredient organic.

sources.title: Viral graphic says North District is now serving fully organic lunches
sources.snippet: A shareable graphic claims every meal ingredient in North District schools is now 100% organic. The graphic cites a district sustainability campaign but includes no procurement document.

sources.title: District food services plan sets 35% organic purchasing target
sources.snippet: The food services plan says the district will raise certified organic purchasing to 35% by spend, with priority on produce, beans, and oats. It states that the district still uses conventional ingredients when organic supply is limited or costs spike.

sources.title: Supplier bulletin details mixed sourcing for school lunch contracts
sources.snippet: The procurement bulletin says North District's new contract includes both organic and conventional product lines. The bulletin specifically lists conventional rice, dairy, and some frozen vegetables among approved items.

claim: Households that installed the city's subsidized heat pumps cut their winter energy bills in half.
notes: Some homes saw large savings, but the citywide median reduction was much smaller.

sources.title: Campaign ad says heat-pump subsidy slashed winter bills by 50%
sources.snippet: An advocacy ad says participating households cut winter energy bills in half after installing subsidized heat pumps. The ad highlights one household that replaced an expensive oil furnace.

sources.title: City evaluation finds median winter bill reduction of 19%
sources.snippet: The city's evaluation of 2,400 households reports a median winter energy bill reduction of 19%, with a wide range depending on insulation, prior fuel type, and electricity prices. A small subgroup of oil-heated homes achieved savings near 50%.

sources.title: Energy research center explains why heat-pump savings vary
sources.snippet: A technical explainer says bill savings depend on the baseline heating system, local tariff design, and envelope efficiency. It warns against presenting upper-end case studies as average program outcomes.

claim: A leaked central bank memo proves cash will be abolished next year and replaced by mandatory digital wallets.
notes: The rumor relies on an unauthenticated memo screenshot and distorted pilot-program language.

sources.title: Channel posts memo claiming physical cash ends next year
sources.snippet: A viral channel shares a screenshot of an alleged central bank memo saying cash will be abolished next year in favor of mandatory digital wallets. The image lacks a signatory block and no official publication link is provided.

sources.title: Central bank says digital-wallet pilot does not replace cash
sources.snippet: The central bank states that its retail digital-currency pilot is optional and does not alter the legal status of cash. It says the memo image circulating online is fabricated and not part of any published consultation.

sources.title: Domain record and corrections history for MarketShock Wire
sources.snippet: The site conceals ownership and has previously deleted false posts about emergency bank closures. Its corrections page shows repeated retractions tied to forged policy documents shared during market-volatility events.

claim: Internal reports prove a new food-packaging chemical causes infertility in humans.
teaching_focus: trace and evidence-quality review before unverifiable verdict
notes: The public record does not authenticate the internal reports or establish human causation.

sources.title: Anonymous leak claims packaging additive causes infertility
sources.snippet: A thread posts screenshots described as internal reports from a packaging supplier and says the additive causes infertility in humans. The screenshots are cropped and omit dates, signatures, and study methods.

sources.title: Chemicals agency says leaked documents are unverified and evidence is preliminary
sources.snippet: The agency says it has not authenticated the circulating screenshots and that current published data are limited to animal and mechanistic studies. It states there is not yet sufficient evidence to conclude the additive causes infertility in humans.

sources.title: Open-source trace links screenshots to a reposted forum image set
sources.snippet: Investigators traced the screenshots to a reposted forum image set whose original upload stripped metadata and cropping context. They say provenance is unresolved and the document chain cannot presently be verified.

claim: This year's Silver Ridge wildfire became the largest in state history, according to the emergency agency's own map.
teaching_focus: distinguish current perimeter from historical ranking
notes: A map screenshot labeled the largest active fire this season, not the largest fire ever recorded.

sources.title: Posts say emergency map proves Silver Ridge is the largest fire ever
sources.snippet: A post shares a screenshot of the state emergency map with the phrase 'largest incident' and claims officials admitted Silver Ridge is the biggest wildfire in state history. The post omits the map legend and timeframe panel.

sources.title: Emergency agency clarifies Silver Ridge is the season's largest active fire
sources.snippet: The agency says the map label referred to the largest active incident in the current season, not an all-time historical ranking. Its historical archive shows two larger fires from prior decades exceeded the current perimeter.

sources.title: Wildfire data guide explains common map-label confusion
sources.snippet: A data guide notes that emergency dashboards often highlight the largest active incident without implying an all-time record. It advises checking archived perimeter tables before making historical claims.
